source.notes: Multiple Claude Code HUD instances each independently polling the usage API, creating additive concurrency burst that exceeds rate limits even when individual usage is low. 5-10+ concurrent windows typical in multi-agent workflows.

hazard.summary: Multiple client instances independently polling the same rate-limited endpoint create additive concurrency burst that exceeds limits — even when individual instance usage is well within limits. Per-instance backoff makes it worse, not better.
signals: 5+ concurrent Claude Code windows each polling usage API independently
429 Too Many Requests on usage/token display endpoint
Per-instance exponential backoff does not help — burst is additive across all instances simultaneously
HUD polls periodically — frequency multiplied by instance count

constraints: Multi-instance polling systems must designate a single poller — not allow each instance to poll independently
CAP classification applies: reduce concurrency (instance count hitting API) before retrying
Per-instance backoff must not be the primary mitigation — it does not reduce total request rate
Shared cache with single designated primary poller must be the default architecture for multi-instance tools
On 429 with no Retry-After, show last known value and reduce poll frequency — do not retry immediately
knobs: poll_frequency_ms — reduce when multiple instances detected
cache_ttl_ms — shared file-based cache TTL for usage data
primary_poller_lock — file lock mechanism to elect single polling instance
jitter_ms — randomize poll offset across instances to prevent synchronized bursts
429_fallback_display — show last known value instead of error on rate limit
verification: 5+ concurrent windows show no 429 errors on usage display
Total API calls per minute does not scale linearly with instance count
Single designated poller confirmed via file lock — others read from cache
On 429, last known value displayed — no error shown to user
Poll frequency reduced automatically when multiple instances detected
notes: This receipt documents a new failure shape: client-side polling amplification. Previous receipts document single-caller 429 misclassification. This case involves correct per-instance behavior that fails predictably at N instances because the burst is additive. The correct classification is CAP — the fix is reducing the number of callers (concurrency), not adding per-caller backoff. Note: CC 2.1.80+ pipes rate_limits via stdin on every render, eliminating the API polling problem entirely for high-frequency data. The receipt generalizes to any multi-instance tool sharing a rate-limited API: monitoring agents, status bars, health checkers, HUD plugins.
